action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: tree, ship, lake, river, farmer, boat, road, bird
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): carry, feed, hear, hold, chase
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a tree carry a ship
    three trees carry a ship

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    three boats feed a ship  =>  puka peki pa nopanu
    two boats feed a lake  =>  nepe peki po nopanu
    three boats feed a tree  =>  nakine peki pa nopanu
    a farmer chase two roads  =>  po tiranu dude kedo
    two ships feed three ships  =>  pa pukanu peki po pukanu
    a road chase three farmers  =>  pa kedonu dude tira
    two trees carry a boat  =>  nopa noni po nakinenu
    three birds carry a ship  =>  puka noni pa retenu
    three rivers carry three lakes  =>  pa nepenu noni pa sunonu
    a ship hear two birds  =>  po retenu kadi puka
    three roads hold three rivers  =>  pa sunonu nikene pa tiranu

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to establish the basic word order (SVO is given, but I need to confirm if there are any post-verbal markers or if the object position is fixed). I also need to determine how the language marks definiteness ('the' vs 'a') and plurality (number words vs bare nouns). I will test sentences with different articles and number words to see if the language repeats the article/number or if it changes based on the verb or subject.

PROBES:
1. a tree carry a ship
2. the tree carry a ship
3. two trees carry a ship
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a tree carry a ship"  =>  puka noni nakine
2. "the tree carry a ship"  =>  puka noni nakine
3. "two trees carry a ship"  =>  puka noni po nakinenu
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The first three probes reveal that the language uses a fixed word order (Subject-Verb-Object) and that the word for "tree" (noni) does not change for definiteness or plurality. The number word "two" (po) appears in the object position and is followed by a plural suffix "-enu" (seen in "po nakinenu"). I need to test if the number word and suffix appear in the subject position as well, and if the article ('the' vs 'a') triggers any changes in the language. I will also test the verb "feed" with different articles to see if the article is repeated or omitted.

PROBES:
1. two trees carry a ship
2. the tree carry a ship
3. a tree feed a lake
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two trees carry a ship"  =>  puka noni po nakinenu
2. "the tree carry a ship"  =>  puka noni nakine
3. "a tree feed a lake"  =>  nepe peki nakine
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The data confirms that the number word and plural suffix (-enu) appear only in the object position when the subject is singular. The article ('the' vs 'a') does not seem to trigger a change in the language for the subject noun (both "a tree" and "the tree" map to "nakine"). However, the verb "feed" (nepe) appears in the object position in probe 3 ("a tree feed a lake" => "nepe peki nakine"), whereas "carry" (noni) appears in the object position in previous probes. This suggests the verb might be moving to the object position or the object position is being filled by the verb itself in some contexts. Wait, looking closer at the structure:
- "a tree carry a ship" => "puka noni nakine" (Ship Verb Tree) -> SVO is NOT the order. It's O-V-S.
- "a tree feed a lake" => "nepe peki nakine" (Lake Verb Tree) -> O-V-S.
- "two trees carry a ship" => "puka noni po nakinenu" (Ship Verb Two Trees) -> O-V-S.
- "three boats feed a ship" => "puka peki pa nopanu" (Ship Verb Three Boats) -> O-V-S.
- "a farmer chase two roads" => "po tiranu dude kedo" (Roads Verb Two Farmers) -> O-V-S.
- "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu" (Ships Verb Three Ships) -> O-V-S.
- "a road chase three farmers" => "pa kedonu dude tira" (Farmers Verb Three Roads) -> O-V-S.
- "two trees carry a boat" => "nopa noni po nakinenu" (Boat Verb Two Trees) -> O-V-S.
- "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu" (Ship Verb Three Birds) -> O-V-S.
- "three rivers carry three lakes" => "pa nepenu noni pa sunonu" (Lakes Verb Three Rivers) -> O-V-S.
- "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka" (Birds Verb Two Ships) -> O-V-S.
- "three roads hold three rivers" => "pa sunonu nikene pa tiranu" (Rivers Verb Three Roads) -> O-V-S.

The word order is consistently **OBJECT - VERB - SUBJECT**.
The verb is always in the middle.
The subject is at the end.
The object is at the beginning.
The number word and plural suffix appear in the subject position (at the end) when the subject is plural.
The article ('the' vs 'a') does not seem to change the form of the nouns in the examples provided so far, but I need to confirm if the article is ever used in the language or if it's just a marker in English. Wait, looking at "a tree feed a lake" => "nepe peki nakine". There is no article in the translation.
Looking at "two trees carry a ship" => "puka noni po nakinenu". No article.
It seems the language might not have articles, or they are omitted.
However, I need to check if the verb changes based on the subject/object.
"carry" (noni) vs "feed" (nepe) vs "chase" (dude) vs "hear" (retenu) vs "hold" (nikene).
The verbs are distinct.
I need to check if the verb agrees with the subject or object in number.
In "two trees carry a ship" (plural subject, singular object) -> "puka noni po nakinenu". Verb "noni" is singular form.
In "three boats feed a ship" (plural subject, singular object) -> "puka peki pa nopanu". Verb "peki" is singular form.
In "two ships feed three ships" (plural subject, plural object) -> "pa pukanu peki po pukanu". Verb "peki" is singular form.
In "three rivers carry three lakes" (plural subject, plural object) -> "pa nepenu noni pa sunonu". Verb "noni" is singular form.
It seems the verb is always in the singular base form, regardless of the number of the subject or object.
I need to check if the number word and suffix appear in the object position.
In "a tree feed a lake" => "nepe peki nakine". Object is "lake" (singular). No number word.
In "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka". Object is "two birds". "puka" is at the end? Wait.
Let's re-examine "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka".
If order is O-V-S:
Object: "two birds"
Verb: "hear" (retenu)
Subject: "a ship"
Translation: "po retenu kadi puka".
"po" = two.
"kadi" = bird?
"puka" = ship?
So "po kadi puka" would be "two birds ship"? No.
Let's look at the structure again.
"po retenu kadi puka".
If O-V-S:
O: "two birds" -> "po kadi"?
V: "hear" -> "retenu"?
S: "a ship" -> "puka"?
This fits: "po kadi retenu puka"? No, the translation is "po retenu kadi puka".
Maybe the order is different?
Let's re-evaluate "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka".
If S-V-O: "a ship hear two birds" => "puka retenu po kadi". No.
If O-V-S: "two birds hear a ship" => "po kadi retenu puka". The translation is "po retenu kadi puka".
Maybe the verb is "retenu" and the object is "kadi puka"? No.
Let's look at "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu".
O: "a ship" -> "puka".
V: "carry" -> "noni".
S: "three birds" -> "pa retenu".
This fits O-V-S perfectly. "puka" (ship) "noni" (carry) "pa retenu" (three birds).
So why is "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka"?
If O-V-S:
O: "two birds" -> "po kadi"?
V: "hear" -> "retenu"?
S: "a ship" -> "puka"?
The translation is "po retenu kadi puka".
This looks like: [two] [hear] [bird] [ship].
This doesn't fit O-V-S if "kadi" is bird and "puka" is ship.
Unless... "kadi" is not bird?
Let's check "a ship hear two birds".
If the order is S-V-O: "a ship hear two birds" => "puka retenu po kadi". No.
If the order is V-O-S: "hear two birds a ship". No.
Maybe the verb "hear" is "retenu" and it's placed differently?
Wait, in "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu".
"puka" = ship.
"noni" = carry.
"pa retenu" = three birds.
So "retenu" is part of the subject phrase "three birds".
Ah! "retenu" might be "bird" + "three"? Or "three" + "bird"?
In "three birds carry a ship", "pa retenu" is the subject. "pa" = three. "retenu" = bird?
If "retenu" = bird, then "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka".
If O-V-S:
O: "two birds" -> "po retenu"?
V: "hear" -> "kadi"?
S: "a ship" -> "puka"?
Then "kadi" = hear.
But in "a ship hear two birds", the translation is "po retenu kadi puka".
If O-V-S:
O: "two birds" -> "po retenu".
V: "hear" -> "kadi".
S: "a ship" -> "puka".
This works if "retenu" = bird and "kadi" = hear.
But earlier I thought "retenu" was "hear" because of "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka".
Let's re-read the examples carefully.
"a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka".
If O-V-S:
O: "two birds" -> "po retenu".
V: "hear" -> "kadi".
S: "a ship" -> "puka".
This implies "retenu" = bird and "kadi" = hear.
But in "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu".
O: "a ship" -> "puka".
V: "carry" -> "noni".
S: "three birds" -> "pa retenu".
This implies "retenu" = bird.
So "retenu" = bird.
Then "kadi" = hear.
Let's check "a ship hear two birds" again.
O: "two birds" -> "po retenu".
V: "hear" -> "kadi".
S: "a ship" -> "puka".
Translation: "po retenu kadi puka".
This matches perfectly!
So the order is **OBJECT - VERB - SUBJECT**.
And the verb is always in the middle.
The subject is at the end.
The object is at the beginning.
The number word and suffix appear in the subject position (at the end) when the subject is plural.
The article ('the' vs 'a') does not seem to appear in the language.
The verb is always in the singular base form.
The number word and suffix appear in the object position when the object is plural?
Let's check "two trees carry a ship" => "puka noni po nakinenu".
O: "a ship" -> "puka".
V: "carry" -> "noni".
S: "two trees" -> "po nakinenu".
Here the number word "po" and suffix "-enu" are in the subject position.
What about "a tree feed a lake" => "nepe peki nakine".
O: "a lake" -> "nepe".
V: "feed" -> "peki".
S: "a tree" -> "nakine".
No number word.
What about "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu".
O: "three ships" -> "pa pukanu".
V: "feed" -> "peki".
S: "two ships" -> "po pukanu".
Here the number word and suffix are in the object position ("pa pukanu") and subject position ("po pukanu").
So the number word and suffix appear in the position of the noun phrase they modify.
The article ('the' vs 'a') is not used in the language.
The verb is always singular.
The word order is O-V-S.

Wait, I need to check if the verb changes based on the number of the subject or object.
In "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu". Verb "peki" is singular.
In "three rivers carry three lakes" => "pa nepenu noni pa sunonu". Verb "noni" is singular.
In "a road chase three farmers" => "pa kedonu dude tira".
O: "three farmers" -> "pa kedonu".
V: "chase" -> "dude".
S: "a road" -> "tira".
Verb "dude" is singular.
In "a farmer chase two roads" => "po tiranu dude kedo".
O: "two roads" -> "po tiranu".
V: "chase" -> "dude".
S: "a farmer" -> "kedo".
Verb "dude" is singular.
So the verb is always singular.

Now I need to check if the number word and suffix appear in the object position when the object is plural.
In "two trees carry a ship" => "puka noni po nakinenu". Object is singular ("a ship"). No number word in object.
In "three boats feed a ship" => "puka peki pa nopanu". Object is singular ("a ship"). No number word in object.
In "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu". Object is singular ("a ship"). No number word in object.
In "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka". Object is plural ("two birds"). Number word "po" is in the object position.
So the number word and suffix appear in the position of the noun phrase they modify.

I need to check if the article ('the' vs 'a') is ever used.
In all examples, there is no article in the translation.
So the language has no articles.
The number word and suffix appear in the position of the noun phrase they modify.
The verb is always singular.
The word order is O-V-S.

I need to check if the verb changes based on the number of the subject or object.
It seems the verb is always singular.

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on the translations, the language follows a strict **Object - Verb - Subject (O-V-S)** word order. The verb is always in the singular base form. There are no articles ('the'/'a') in the target language; instead, number is marked by the number word (two, three) and a plural suffix (-enu) attached to the noun in the position it appears. The noun itself does not change form for definiteness or number (e.g., "tree" is "noni" in both singular and plural contexts, with the number word/suffix handling the count). I need to confirm the plural suffix behavior with a verb that has a different number of subjects/objects to ensure the suffix attaches to the noun regardless of position, and verify that the number word always precedes the noun. I will test a sentence with a plural subject and a plural object to see if the suffix appears on both nouns.

PROBES:
1. two trees feed two lakes
2. a farmer feed three birds
3. three roads hold two rivers
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two trees feed two lakes"  =>  po nepenu peki po nakinenu
2. "a farmer feed three birds"  =>  pa retenu peki kedo
3. "three roads hold two rivers"  =>  po sunonu nikene pa tiranu
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 9 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The grammar is now fully established:
- **Word Order**: Object - Verb - Subject (O-V-S).
- **Verb**: Always singular base form, placed in the middle.
- **Number/Plurality**: Marked by a number word (two, three) + a plural suffix (-enu) attached to the noun. This combination appears in the position of the noun phrase (Object or Subject).
- **Articles**: No articles ('the'/'a') are used in the target language.
- **Noun Forms**: Nouns do not change form for definiteness or number; only the number word and suffix are added.

I have 3 probes left. I will use them to confirm the specific words for the remaining nouns/verbs not yet explicitly isolated in a singular/plural pair (though most are covered), and to ensure the rule holds for the final edge cases. Specifically, I want to confirm the word for "river" (nepu) and "road" (kedo) in singular form, and verify the verb "hold" (nikene) and "chase" (dude) are indeed singular. I'll also test a sentence with "three" to ensure the suffix "-enu" is consistent.

PROBES:
1. a river feed a tree
2. a road carry a bird
3. three birds hold three roads
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a river feed a tree"  =>  nakine peki suno
2. "a road carry a bird"  =>  rete noni tira
3. "three birds hold three roads"  =>  pa tiranu nikene pa retenu

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. three boats feed a river
2. a tree chase three birds
3. two farmers chase three birds
4. three boats hear a river
5. two boats chase a farmer
6. three ships carry two trees
7. two birds carry three trees
8. a river hold two lakes
9. two trees hear two boats
10. three farmers hold a farmer

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on the established grammar (O-V-S word order, singular verb, number word + -enu suffix on the noun in its position, no articles), I will translate each sentence:
1. three boats feed a river: Object (three boats) + Verb (feed) + Subject (a river). "pa nopanu peki suno".
2. a tree chase three birds: Object (three birds) + Verb (chase) + Subject (a tree). "pa retenu dude nakine".
3. two farmers chase three birds: Object (three birds) + Verb (chase) + Subject (two farmers). "pa retenu dude pa tiranu".
4. three boats hear a river: Object (a river) + Verb (hear) + Subject (three boats). "suno retenu pa nopanu".
5. two boats chase a farmer: Object (a farmer) + Verb (chase) + Subject (two boats). "kedo dude pa nopanu".
6. three ships carry two trees: Object (two trees) + Verb (carry) + Subject (three ships). "po nakinenu noni pa pukanu".
7. two birds carry three trees: Object (three trees) + Verb (carry) + Subject (two birds). "pa nakinenu noni pa retenu".
8. a river hold two lakes: Object (two lakes) + Verb (hold) + Subject (a river). "po sunonu nikene nakine".
9. two trees hear two boats: Object (two boats) + Verb (hear) + Subject (two trees). "pa nopanu retenu po nakinenu".
10. three farmers hold a farmer: Object (a farmer) + Verb (hold) + Subject (three farmers). "kedo nikene pa tiranu".

ANSWERS:
1. pa nopanu peki suno
2. pa retenu dude nakine
3. pa retenu dude pa tiranu
4. suno retenu pa nopanu
5. kedo dude pa nopanu
6. po nakinenu noni pa pukanu
7. pa nakinenu noni pa retenu
8. po sunonu nikene nakine
9. pa nopanu retenu po nakinenu
10. kedo nikene pa tiranu
action.label: response
